profile.summary: Owns product strategy, portfolio priorities, and product team performance across multiple areas. Translates company goals into roadmap choices, manages product leaders or senior PMs, and works closely with engineering, design, data, go-to-market, and executives. Balances customer value, commercial outcomes, delivery risk, and team health while shaping how product decisions get made.

known_challenges.text: Balancing long-term product strategy with short-term revenue, delivery, and executive pressure.
Keeping multiple teams aligned without becoming the bottleneck for every decision.
Making trade-offs across a portfolio when data is incomplete or teams compete for priority.
Developing product managers and product leaders while still carrying high-stakes business decisions.
Sustaining energy under constant context switching, stakeholder demands, and visible accountability.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role is judged on whether the right bets are being made across teams, not just within one roadmap.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Decision quality with executives and peers
recommended_question_themes.why: A Head of Product often succeeds or stalls based on how well they influence across engineering, commercial, and leadership groups.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Health and growth of the product organisation
recommended_question_themes.why: They usually own hiring, coaching, role clarity, and the operating standard for PMs.
This role constantly has to protect product judgment from becoming purely reactive to loud stakeholders.
The role carries visible accountability and frequent context switching, which can quietly erode judgment and resilience.

terminology.meaning: The choices about where to play and why.
Sequenced product bets and delivery direction.
Work to understand problems before building.
Measures tied to user or business results.
The full set of product investments.
How product teams make decisions and work.
How PM roles, teams, and ownership are structured.
Helping PMs improve judgment and execution.
Handling expectations, input, and alignment across groups.
An issue raised for faster or higher-level resolution.
Shared understanding with senior leaders on direction.
Cross-team planning for the next quarter.
How a product is launched and sold.
A key measure of product value delivered.
Regular forum to inspect progress and decisions.
Using AI to speed synthesis or exploration.

listen_for: Whether they talk in terms of portfolio choices or get pulled into single-team detail
How they describe trade-offs between customer value, revenue, and delivery constraints
Whether they name people-development issues as clearly as roadmap issues
Shifts from evidence-based language to stakeholder-driven language under pressure
Signs they are carrying too many decisions personally
avoid: Treating them like the most senior individual PM rather than an organisational leader
Pushing for more roadmap detail when the real issue is strategy, org design, or decision clarity
Assuming stakeholder conflict means poor alignment skills rather than genuine business trade-offs
Focusing only on delivery output and ignoring team quality, product judgment, and portfolio health
